categories.name: A First Set of Refactorings
categories.description: The most useful and commonly applied refactorings that form the foundation of the catalog.

refactorings.name: Extract Function
refactorings.description: Extract Function is one of the most common refactorings I do. (Here, I use the term "function" but the same is true for a method in an object-oriented language, or any kind of procedure or subroutine.) I look at a fragment of code, understand what it is doing, then extract it into its own function named after its purpose.
refactorings.motivation: Extract Function is one of the most common refactorings I do. (Here, I use the term "function" but the same is true for a method in an object-oriented language, or any kind of procedure or subroutine.) I look at a fragment of code, understand what it is doing, then extract it into its own function named after its purpose.

refactorings.name: Inline Function
refactorings.description: One of the themes of this book is using short functions named to show their intent, because these functions lead to clearer and easier to read code. But sometimes, I do come across a function in which the body is as clear as the name.
refactorings.motivation: One of the themes of this book is using short functions named to show their intent, because these functions lead to clearer and easier to read code. But sometimes, I do come across a function in which the body is as clear as the name.

refactorings.name: Extract Variable
refactorings.description: Expressions can become very complex and hard to read. In such situations, local variables may help break the expression down into something more manageable. In particular, they give me an ability to name a part of a more complex piece of logic.
refactorings.motivation: Expressions can become very complex and hard to read. In such situations, local variables may help break the expression down into something more manageable. In particular, they give me an ability to name a part of a more complex piece of logic.

refactorings.name: Inline Variable
refactorings.description: Variables provide names for expressions within a function, and as such they are usually a Good Thing. But sometimes, the name doesn't really communicate more than the expression itself. At other times, you may find that a variable gets in the way of refactoring the neighboring code.
refactorings.motivation: Variables provide names for expressions within a function, and as such they are usually a Good Thing. But sometimes, the name doesn't really communicate more than the expression itself. At other times, you may find that a variable gets in the way of refactoring the neighboring code.

refactorings.name: Change Function Declaration
refactorings.description: Functions represent the primary way we break a program down into parts. Function declarations represent how these parts fit together -- effectively, they represent the joints in our software systems. And, as with any construction, much depends on those joints.
refactorings.motivation: Functions represent the primary way we break a program down into parts. Function declarations represent how these parts fit together -- effectively, they represent the joints in our software systems. And, as with any construction, much depends on those joints.

refactorings.name: Encapsulate Variable
refactorings.description: Refactoring is all about manipulating the elements of our programs. Data is more awkward to manipulate than functions. Since using a function usually means calling it, I can easily rename or move a function while keeping the old function intact as a forwarding function (so my old code calls the old function, which calls the new function).
refactorings.motivation: Refactoring is all about manipulating the elements of our programs. Data is more awkward to manipulate than functions. Since using a function usually means calling it, I can easily rename or move a function while keeping the old function intact as a forwarding function (so my old code calls the old function, which calls the new function).

refactorings.name: Rename Variable
refactorings.description: Naming things well is the heart of clear programming. Variables can do a lot to explain what I'm up to -- if I name them well. But I frequently get my names wrong -- sometimes because I'm not thinking carefully enough, sometimes because my understanding of the problem improves as I learn more, and sometimes because the program's purpose changes as my users' needs change.
refactorings.motivation: Naming things well is the heart of clear programming. Variables can do a lot to explain what I'm up to -- if I name them well. But I frequently get my names wrong -- sometimes because I'm not thinking carefully enough, sometimes because my understanding of the problem improves as I learn more, and sometimes because the program's purpose changes as my users' needs change.

refactorings.name: Introduce Parameter Object
refactorings.description: I often see groups of data items that regularly travel together, appearing in function after function. Such a group is a data clump, and I like to replace it with a single data structure. Grouping data into a structure is valuable because it makes explicit the relationship between the data items.
refactorings.motivation: I often see groups of data items that regularly travel together, appearing in function after function. Such a group is a data clump, and I like to replace it with a single data structure. Grouping data into a structure is valuable because it makes explicit the relationship between the data items.

refactorings.name: Combine Functions into Class
refactorings.description: Classes are a fundamental construct in most modern programming languages. They bind together data and functions into a shared environment, exposing some of that data and function to other program elements for collaboration.
refactorings.motivation: Classes are a fundamental construct in most modern programming languages. They bind together data and functions into a shared environment, exposing some of that data and function to other program elements for collaboration.

refactorings.name: Combine Functions into Transform
refactorings.description: Software often involves feeding data into programs that calculate various derived information from it. These derived values may be needed in several places, and those calculations are often repeated wherever the derived data is used.
refactorings.motivation: Software often involves feeding data into programs that calculate various derived information from it. These derived values may be needed in several places, and those calculations are often repeated wherever the derived data is used.

refactorings.name: Split Phase
refactorings.description: When I run into code that's dealing with two different things, I look for a way to split it into separate modules. I endeavor to make this split because, if I need to make a change, I can deal with each topic separately and not have to hold both in my head together.
refactorings.motivation: When I run into code that's dealing with two different things, I look for a way to split it into separate modules. I endeavor to make this split because, if I need to make a change, I can deal with each topic separately and not have to hold both in my head together.

categories.name: Encapsulation
categories.description: Refactorings that help hide implementation details and manage data access.

refactorings.name: Encapsulate Record
refactorings.description: This is why I often favor objects over records for mutable data. With objects, I can hide what is stored and provide methods for all three values. The user of the object doesn't need to know or care which is stored and which is calculated.
refactorings.motivation: This is why I often favor objects over records for mutable data. With objects, I can hide what is stored and provide methods for all three values. The user of the object doesn't need to know or care which is stored and which is calculated.

refactorings.name: Encapsulate Collection
refactorings.description: I like encapsulating any mutable data in my programs. This makes it easier to see when and how data structures are modified, which then makes it easier to change those data structures when I need to. Encapsulation is often encouraged, particularly by object-oriented developers, but a common mistake occurs when working with collections.
refactorings.motivation: I like encapsulating any mutable data in my programs. This makes it easier to see when and how data structures are modified, which then makes it easier to change those data structures when I need to. Encapsulation is often encouraged, particularly by object-oriented developers, but a common mistake occurs when working with collections.

refactorings.name: Replace Primitive with Object
refactorings.description: Often, in early stages of development you make decisions about representing simple facts as simple data items, such as numbers or strings. As development proceeds, those simple items aren't so simple anymore.
refactorings.motivation: Often, in early stages of development you make decisions about representing simple facts as simple data items, such as numbers or strings. As development proceeds, those simple items aren't so simple anymore.

refactorings.name: Replace Temp with Query
refactorings.description: One use of temporary variables is to capture the value of some code in order to refer to it later in a function. Using a temp allows me to refer to the value while explaining its meaning and avoiding repeating the code that calculates it.
refactorings.motivation: One use of temporary variables is to capture the value of some code in order to refer to it later in a function. Using a temp allows me to refer to the value while explaining its meaning and avoiding repeating the code that calculates it.

refactorings.name: Extract Class
refactorings.description: You've probably read guidelines that a class should be a crisp abstraction, only handle a few clear responsibilities, and so on. In practice, classes grow. You add some operations here, a bit of data there.
refactorings.motivation: You've probably read guidelines that a class should be a crisp abstraction, only handle a few clear responsibilities, and so on. In practice, classes grow. You add some operations here, a bit of data there.

refactorings.name: Inline Class
refactorings.description: Inline Class is the inverse of Extract Class (182). I use Inline Class if a class is no longer pulling its weight and shouldn't be around any more. Often, this is the result of refactoring that moves other responsibilities out of the class so there is little left.
refactorings.motivation: Inline Class is the inverse of Extract Class (182). I use Inline Class if a class is no longer pulling its weight and shouldn't be around any more. Often, this is the result of refactoring that moves other responsibilities out of the class so there is little left.

refactorings.name: Hide Delegate
refactorings.description: One of the keys -- if not the key -- to good modular design is encapsulation. Encapsulation means that modules need to know less about other parts of the system. Then, when things change, fewer modules need to be told about the change -- which makes the change easier to make.
refactorings.motivation: One of the keys -- if not the key -- to good modular design is encapsulation. Encapsulation means that modules need to know less about other parts of the system. Then, when things change, fewer modules need to be told about the change -- which makes the change easier to make.

refactorings.name: Remove Middle Man
refactorings.description: In the motivation for Hide Delegate (189), I talked about the advantages of encapsulating the use of a delegated object. There is a price for this. Every time the client wants to use a new feature of the delegate, I have to add a simple delegating method to the server.
refactorings.motivation: In the motivation for Hide Delegate (189), I talked about the advantages of encapsulating the use of a delegated object. There is a price for this. Every time the client wants to use a new feature of the delegate, I have to add a simple delegating method to the server.

refactorings.name: Substitute Algorithm
refactorings.description: I've never tried to skin a cat. I'm told there are several ways to do it. I'm sure some are easier than others.
refactorings.motivation: I've never tried to skin a cat. I'm told there are several ways to do it. I'm sure some are easier than others.

categories.name: Moving Features
categories.description: Refactorings for moving elements between contexts to improve code organization.

refactorings.name: Move Function
refactorings.description: The heart of a good software design is its modularity -- which is my ability to make most modifications to a program while only having to understand a small part of it. To get this modularity, I need to ensure that related software elements are grouped together and the links between them are easy to find and understand.
refactorings.motivation: The heart of a good software design is its modularity -- which is my ability to make most modifications to a program while only having to understand a small part of it. To get this modularity, I need to ensure that related software elements are grouped together and the links between them are easy to find and understand.

refactorings.name: Move Field
refactorings.description: Programming involves writing a lot of code that implements behavior -- but the strength of a program is really founded on its data structures. If I have a good set of data structures that match the problem, then my behavior code is simple and straightforward.
refactorings.motivation: Programming involves writing a lot of code that implements behavior -- but the strength of a program is really founded on its data structures. If I have a good set of data structures that match the problem, then my behavior code is simple and straightforward.

refactorings.name: Move Statements into Function
refactorings.description: Removing duplication is one of the best rules of thumb of healthy code. If I see the same code executed every time I call a particular function, I look to combine that repeating code into the function itself.
refactorings.motivation: Removing duplication is one of the best rules of thumb of healthy code. If I see the same code executed every time I call a particular function, I look to combine that repeating code into the function itself.

refactorings.name: Move Statements to Callers
refactorings.description: Functions are the basic building block of the abstractions we build as programmers. And, as with any abstraction, we don't always get the boundaries right. As a code base changes its capabilities -- as most useful software does -- we often find our abstraction boundaries shift.
refactorings.motivation: Functions are the basic building block of the abstractions we build as programmers. And, as with any abstraction, we don't always get the boundaries right. As a code base changes its capabilities -- as most useful software does -- we often find our abstraction boundaries shift.

refactorings.name: Replace Inline Code with Function Call
refactorings.description: Functions allow me to package up bits of behavior. This is useful for understanding -- a named function can explain the purpose of the code rather than its mechanics. It's also valuable to remove duplication: Instead of writing the same code twice, I just call the function.
refactorings.motivation: Functions allow me to package up bits of behavior. This is useful for understanding -- a named function can explain the purpose of the code rather than its mechanics. It's also valuable to remove duplication: Instead of writing the same code twice, I just call the function.

refactorings.name: Slide Statements
refactorings.description: Code is easier to understand when things that are related to each other appear together. If several lines of code access the same data structure, it's best for them to be together rather than intermingled with code accessing other data structures.
refactorings.motivation: Code is easier to understand when things that are related to each other appear together. If several lines of code access the same data structure, it's best for them to be together rather than intermingled with code accessing other data structures.

refactorings.name: Split Loop
refactorings.description: You often see loops that are doing two different things at once just because they can do that with one pass through a loop. But if you're doing two different things in the same loop, then whenever you need to modify the loop you have to understand both things.
refactorings.motivation: You often see loops that are doing two different things at once just because they can do that with one pass through a loop. But if you're doing two different things in the same loop, then whenever you need to modify the loop you have to understand both things.

refactorings.name: Replace Loop with Pipeline
refactorings.description: Like most programmers, I was taught to use loops to iterate over a collection of objects. Increasingly, however, language environments provide a better construct: the collection pipeline. Collection Pipelines [mf-cp] allow me to describe my processing as a series of operations, each consuming and emitting a collection.
refactorings.motivation: Like most programmers, I was taught to use loops to iterate over a collection of objects. Increasingly, however, language environments provide a better construct: the collection pipeline. Collection Pipelines [mf-cp] allow me to describe my processing as a series of operations, each consuming and emitting a collection.

refactorings.name: Remove Dead Code
refactorings.description: When we put code into production, even on people's devices, we aren't charged by weight. A few unused lines of code don't slow down our systems nor take up significant memory; indeed, decent compilers will instinctively remove them.
refactorings.motivation: When we put code into production, even on people's devices, we aren't charged by weight. A few unused lines of code don't slow down our systems nor take up significant memory; indeed, decent compilers will instinctively remove them.

categories.name: Organizing Data
categories.description: Refactorings that help organize data structures and clarify their roles.

refactorings.name: Split Variable
refactorings.description: Variables have various uses. Some of these uses naturally lead to the variable being assigned to several times. Loop variables change for each run of a loop (such as the i in for (let i=0; i<10; i++)).
refactorings.motivation: Variables have various uses. Some of these uses naturally lead to the variable being assigned to several times. Loop variables change for each run of a loop (such as the i in for (let i=0; i<10; i++)).

refactorings.name: Rename Field
refactorings.description: Names are important, and field names in record structures can be especially important when those record structures are widely used across a program. Data structures play a particularly important role in understanding.
refactorings.motivation: Names are important, and field names in record structures can be especially important when those record structures are widely used across a program. Data structures play a particularly important role in understanding.

refactorings.name: Replace Derived Variable with Query
refactorings.description: One of the biggest sources of problems in software is mutable data. Data changes can often couple together parts of code in awkward ways, with changes in one part leading to knock-on effects that are hard to spot.
refactorings.motivation: One of the biggest sources of problems in software is mutable data. Data changes can often couple together parts of code in awkward ways, with changes in one part leading to knock-on effects that are hard to spot.

refactorings.name: Change Reference to Value
refactorings.description: When I nest an object, or data structure, within another I can treat the inner object as a reference or as a value. The difference is most obviously visible in how I handle updates of the inner object's properties.
refactorings.motivation: When I nest an object, or data structure, within another I can treat the inner object as a reference or as a value. The difference is most obviously visible in how I handle updates of the inner object's properties.

refactorings.name: Change Value to Reference
refactorings.description: A data structure may have several records linked to the same logical data structure. I might read in a list of orders, some of which are for the same customer. When I have sharing like this, I can represent it by treating the customer either as a value or as a reference.
refactorings.motivation: A data structure may have several records linked to the same logical data structure. I might read in a list of orders, some of which are for the same customer. When I have sharing like this, I can represent it by treating the customer either as a value or as a reference.

categories.name: Simplifying Conditional Logic
categories.description: Refactorings that clarify and simplify conditional expressions and control flow.

refactorings.name: Decompose Conditional
refactorings.description: One of the most common sources of complexity in a program is complex conditional logic. As I write code to do various things depending on various conditions, I can quickly end up with a pretty long function.
refactorings.motivation: One of the most common sources of complexity in a program is complex conditional logic. As I write code to do various things depending on various conditions, I can quickly end up with a pretty long function.

refactorings.name: Consolidate Conditional Expression
refactorings.description: Sometimes, I run into a series of conditional checks where each check is different yet the resulting action is the same. When I see this, I use and and or operators to consolidate them into a single conditional check with a single result.
refactorings.motivation: Sometimes, I run into a series of conditional checks where each check is different yet the resulting action is the same. When I see this, I use and and or operators to consolidate them into a single conditional check with a single result.

refactorings.name: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses
refactorings.description: I often find that conditional expressions come in two styles. In the first style, both legs of the conditional are part of normal behavior, while in the second style, one leg is normal and the other indicates an unusual condition.
refactorings.motivation: I often find that conditional expressions come in two styles. In the first style, both legs of the conditional are part of normal behavior, while in the second style, one leg is normal and the other indicates an unusual condition.

refactorings.name: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism
refactorings.description: Complex conditional logic is one of the hardest things to reason about in programming, so I always look for ways to add structure to conditional logic. Often, I find I can separate the logic into different circumstances -- high-level cases -- to divide the conditions.
refactorings.motivation: Complex conditional logic is one of the hardest things to reason about in programming, so I always look for ways to add structure to conditional logic. Often, I find I can separate the logic into different circumstances -- high-level cases -- to divide the conditions.

refactorings.name: Introduce Special Case
refactorings.description: A common case of duplicated code is when many users of a data structure check a specific value, and then most of them do the same thing. If I find many parts of the code base having the same reaction to a particular value, I want to bring that reaction into a single place.
refactorings.motivation: A common case of duplicated code is when many users of a data structure check a specific value, and then most of them do the same thing. If I find many parts of the code base having the same reaction to a particular value, I want to bring that reaction into a single place.

refactorings.name: Introduce Assertion
refactorings.description: Often, sections of code work only if certain conditions are true. This may be as simple as a square root calculation only working on a positive input value. With an object, it may require that at least one of a group of fields has a value in it.
refactorings.motivation: Often, sections of code work only if certain conditions are true. This may be as simple as a square root calculation only working on a positive input value. With an object, it may require that at least one of a group of fields has a value in it.

categories.name: Refactoring APIs
categories.description: Refactorings that improve module and function interfaces.

refactorings.name: Separate Query from Modifier
refactorings.description: When I have a function that gives me a value and has no observable side effects, I have a very valuable thing. I can call this function as often as I like. I can move the call to other places in a calling function.
refactorings.motivation: When I have a function that gives me a value and has no observable side effects, I have a very valuable thing. I can call this function as often as I like. I can move the call to other places in a calling function.

refactorings.name: Parameterize Function
refactorings.description: If I see two functions that carry out very similar logic with different literal values, I can remove the duplication by using a single function with parameters for the different values. This increases the usefulness of the function, since I can apply it elsewhere with different values.
refactorings.motivation: If I see two functions that carry out very similar logic with different literal values, I can remove the duplication by using a single function with parameters for the different values. This increases the usefulness of the function, since I can apply it elsewhere with different values.

refactorings.name: Remove Flag Argument
refactorings.description: A flag argument is a function argument that the caller uses to indicate which logic the called function should execute. I may call a function that looks like this: Click here to view code image function bookConcert(aCustomer, isPremium) { if (isPremium) { // logic for premium booking } else { // logic for regular booking } } To book a premium concert, I issue the call like so: bookConcert(aCustomer, true); Flag arguments can also come as enums: Click here to view code image bookConcert(aCustomer, CustomerType.PREMIUM); or strings (or symbols in languages that use them): Click here to view code image bookConcert(aCustomer, "premium"); I dislike flag arguments because they complicate the process of understanding what function calls are available and how to call them.
refactorings.motivation: A flag argument is a function argument that the caller uses to indicate which logic the called function should execute. I may call a function that looks like this: Click here to view code image function bookConcert(aCustomer, isPremium) { if (isPremium) { // logic for premium booking } else { // logic for regular booking } } To book a premium concert, I issue the call like so: bookConcert(aCustomer, true); Flag arguments can also come as enums: Click here to view code image bookConcert(aCustomer, CustomerType.PREMIUM); or strings (or symbols in languages that use them): Click here to view code image bookConcert(aCustomer, "premium"); I dislike flag arguments because they complicate the process of understanding what function calls are available and how to call them.

refactorings.name: Preserve Whole Object
refactorings.description: If I see code that derives a couple of values from a record and then passes these values into a function, I like to replace those values with the whole record itself, letting the function body derive the values it needs. Passing the whole record handles change better should the called function need more data from the whole in the future -- that change would not require me to alter the parameter list.
refactorings.motivation: If I see code that derives a couple of values from a record and then passes these values into a function, I like to replace those values with the whole record itself, letting the function body derive the values it needs. Passing the whole record handles change better should the called function need more data from the whole in the future -- that change would not require me to alter the parameter list.

refactorings.name: Replace Parameter with Query
refactorings.description: The parameter list to a function should summarize the points of variability of that function, indicating the primary ways in which that function may behave differently. As with any statement in code, it's good to avoid any duplication, and it's easier to understand if the parameter list is short.
refactorings.motivation: The parameter list to a function should summarize the points of variability of that function, indicating the primary ways in which that function may behave differently. As with any statement in code, it's good to avoid any duplication, and it's easier to understand if the parameter list is short.

refactorings.name: Replace Query with Parameter
refactorings.description: When looking through a function's body, I sometimes see references to something in the function's scope that I'm not happy with. This might be a reference to a global variable, or to an element in the same module that I intend to move away.
refactorings.motivation: When looking through a function's body, I sometimes see references to something in the function's scope that I'm not happy with. This might be a reference to a global variable, or to an element in the same module that I intend to move away.

refactorings.name: Remove Setting Method
refactorings.description: Providing a setting method indicates that a field may be changed. If I don't want that field to change once the object is created, I don't provide a setting method (and make the field immutable). That way, the field is set only in the constructor, my intention to have it not change is clear, and I usually remove the very possibility that the field will change.
refactorings.motivation: Providing a setting method indicates that a field may be changed. If I don't want that field to change once the object is created, I don't provide a setting method (and make the field immutable). That way, the field is set only in the constructor, my intention to have it not change is clear, and I usually remove the very possibility that the field will change.

refactorings.name: Replace Constructor with Factory Function
refactorings.description: Many object-oriented languages have a special constructor function that's called to initialize an object. Clients typically call this constructor when they want to create a new object. But these constructors often come with awkward limitations that aren't there for more general functions.
refactorings.motivation: Many object-oriented languages have a special constructor function that's called to initialize an object. Clients typically call this constructor when they want to create a new object. But these constructors often come with awkward limitations that aren't there for more general functions.

refactorings.name: Replace Function with Command
refactorings.description: Functions -- either freestanding or attached to objects as methods -- are one of the fundamental building blocks of programming. But there are times when it's useful to encapsulate a function into its own object, which I refer to as a "command object" or simply a command.
refactorings.motivation: Functions -- either freestanding or attached to objects as methods -- are one of the fundamental building blocks of programming. But there are times when it's useful to encapsulate a function into its own object, which I refer to as a "command object" or simply a command.

refactorings.name: Replace Command with Function
refactorings.description: Command objects provide a powerful mechanism for handling complex computations. They can easily be broken down into separate methods sharing common state through the fields; they can be invoked via different methods for different effects; they can have their data built up in stages.
refactorings.motivation: Command objects provide a powerful mechanism for handling complex computations. They can easily be broken down into separate methods sharing common state through the fields; they can be invoked via different methods for different effects; they can have their data built up in stages.

refactorings.name: Pull Up Method
refactorings.description: Eliminating duplicate code is important. Two duplicate methods may work fine as they are, but they are nothing but a breeding ground for bugs in the future. Whenever there is duplication, there is risk that an alteration to one copy will not be made to the other.
refactorings.motivation: Eliminating duplicate code is important. Two duplicate methods may work fine as they are, but they are nothing but a breeding ground for bugs in the future. Whenever there is duplication, there is risk that an alteration to one copy will not be made to the other.

refactorings.name: Pull Up Field
refactorings.description: If subclasses are developed independently, or combined through refactoring, I often find that they duplicate features. In particular, certain fields can be duplicates. Such fields sometimes have similar names -- but not always.
refactorings.motivation: If subclasses are developed independently, or combined through refactoring, I often find that they duplicate features. In particular, certain fields can be duplicates. Such fields sometimes have similar names -- but not always.

refactorings.name: Pull Up Constructor Body
refactorings.description: Constructors are tricky things. They aren't quite normal methods -- so I'm more restricted in what I can do with them. If I see subclass methods with common behavior, my first thought is to use Extract Function (106) followed by Pull Up Method (350), which will move it nicely into the superclass.
refactorings.motivation: Constructors are tricky things. They aren't quite normal methods -- so I'm more restricted in what I can do with them. If I see subclass methods with common behavior, my first thought is to use Extract Function (106) followed by Pull Up Method (350), which will move it nicely into the superclass.

refactorings.name: Push Down Method
refactorings.description: If a method is only relevant to one subclass (or a small proportion of subclasses), removing it from the superclass and putting it only on the subclass(es) makes that clearer. I can only do this refactoring if the caller knows it's working with a particular subclass -- otherwise, I should use Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (272) with some placebo behavior on the superclass.
refactorings.motivation: If a method is only relevant to one subclass (or a small proportion of subclasses), removing it from the superclass and putting it only on the subclass(es) makes that clearer. I can only do this refactoring if the caller knows it's working with a particular subclass -- otherwise, I should use Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (272) with some placebo behavior on the superclass.

refactorings.name: Push Down Field
refactorings.description: If a field is only used by one subclass (or a small proportion of subclasses), I move it to those subclasses.
refactorings.motivation: If a field is only used by one subclass (or a small proportion of subclasses), I move it to those subclasses.

refactorings.name: Replace Type Code with Subclasses
refactorings.description: Software systems often need to represent different kinds of a similar thing. I may classify employees by their job type (engineer, manager, salesman), or orders by their priority (rush, regular). My first tool for handling this is some kind of type code field -- depending on the language, that might be an enum, symbol, string, or number.
refactorings.motivation: Software systems often need to represent different kinds of a similar thing. I may classify employees by their job type (engineer, manager, salesman), or orders by their priority (rush, regular). My first tool for handling this is some kind of type code field -- depending on the language, that might be an enum, symbol, string, or number.

refactorings.name: Remove Subclass
refactorings.description: Subclasses are useful. They support variations in data structure and polymorphic behavior. They are a good way to program by difference.
refactorings.motivation: Subclasses are useful. They support variations in data structure and polymorphic behavior. They are a good way to program by difference.

refactorings.name: Extract Superclass
refactorings.description: If I see two classes doing similar things, I can take advantage of the basic mechanism of inheritance to pull their similarities together into a superclass. I can use Pull Up Field (353) to move common data into the superclass, and Pull Up Method (350) to move the common behavior.
refactorings.motivation: If I see two classes doing similar things, I can take advantage of the basic mechanism of inheritance to pull their similarities together into a superclass. I can use Pull Up Field (353) to move common data into the superclass, and Pull Up Method (350) to move the common behavior.

refactorings.name: Collapse Hierarchy
refactorings.description: When I'm refactoring a class hierarchy, I'm often pulling and pushing features around. As the hierarchy evolves, I sometimes find that a class and its parent are no longer different enough to be worth keeping separate.
refactorings.motivation: When I'm refactoring a class hierarchy, I'm often pulling and pushing features around. As the hierarchy evolves, I sometimes find that a class and its parent are no longer different enough to be worth keeping separate.

refactorings.name: Replace Subclass with Delegate
refactorings.description: If I have some objects whose behavior varies from category to category, the natural mechanism to express this is inheritance. I put all the common data and behavior in the superclass, and let each subclass add and override features as needed.
refactorings.motivation: If I have some objects whose behavior varies from category to category, the natural mechanism to express this is inheritance. I put all the common data and behavior in the superclass, and let each subclass add and override features as needed.

refactorings.name: Replace Superclass with Delegate
refactorings.description: In object-oriented programs, inheritance is a powerful and easily available way to reuse existing functionality. I inherit from some existing class, then override and add additional features. But subclassing can be done in a way that leads to confusion and complication.
refactorings.motivation: In object-oriented programs, inheritance is a powerful and easily available way to reuse existing functionality. I inherit from some existing class, then override and add additional features. But subclassing can be done in a way that leads to confusion and complication.

smells.name: Mysterious Name
smells.description: Code elements (functions, variables, classes) whose names don't clearly communicate what they do, forcing readers to puzzle over the code.

smells.name: Duplicated Code
smells.description: The same code structure appears in more than one place, meaning changes must be made in multiple locations and readers must carefully check for differences.

smells.name: Long Function
smells.description: Functions that are too long, making them difficult to understand. Short functions with good names are much easier to follow.

smells.name: Long Parameter List
smells.description: Functions with too many parameters, making them confusing to call and understand.

smells.name: Global Data
smells.description: Data that can be modified from anywhere in the code base with no mechanism to discover which code touched it, leading to hard-to-find bugs.

smells.name: Mutable Data
smells.description: Data changes that lead to unexpected consequences and tricky bugs, especially when different parts of the software expect different values.

smells.name: Divergent Change
smells.description: A module that is often changed in different ways for different reasons, indicating it has multiple responsibilities that should be separated.

smells.name: Shotgun Surgery
smells.description: A change that requires many small edits to many different classes, making changes hard to find and easy to miss.

smells.name: Feature Envy
smells.description: A function that spends more time communicating with functions or data of another module than with its own, suggesting it belongs elsewhere.

smells.name: Data Clumps
smells.description: Groups of data items that repeatedly appear together in fields and parameter lists, suggesting they should be combined into their own object.

smells.name: Primitive Obsession
smells.description: Using primitive types (strings, numbers) instead of small domain objects for concepts like money, coordinates, or ranges.

smells.name: Repeated Switches
smells.description: The same conditional switching logic (switch/case or if/else cascades) appearing in multiple places, requiring updates everywhere when a new clause is added.

smells.name: Loops
smells.description: Traditional loop constructs that can be replaced with more expressive pipeline operations like filter and map.

smells.name: Lazy Element
smells.description: A program element (function or class) that doesn't do enough to justify its existence, such as a function named the same as its body or a class with a single simple function.

smells.name: Speculative Generality
smells.description: Hooks, special cases, and abstract classes added for imagined future needs that aren't currently required, making the code harder to understand and maintain.

smells.name: Temporary Field
smells.description: A field in a class that is only set in certain circumstances, making the class confusing because you expect objects to use all their fields.

smells.name: Message Chains
smells.description: A client navigates through a chain of objects (a.getB().getC().getD()), coupling it to the structure of that navigation chain.

smells.name: Middle Man
smells.description: A class where half the methods simply delegate to another class, providing no added value and obscuring the real logic.

smells.name: Insider Trading
smells.description: Modules that trade too much data between each other, increasing coupling beyond what is necessary.

smells.name: Large Class
smells.description: A class that is trying to do too much, with too many fields or too much code, breeding duplicated code.

smells.name: Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces
smells.description: Classes that could be substituted for each other but have different interfaces, preventing polymorphic use.

smells.name: Data Class
smells.description: Classes that have nothing but fields and getters/setters, being manipulated in too much detail by other classes instead of having their own behavior.

smells.name: Refused Bequest
smells.description: A subclass that inherits methods and data from a parent but doesn't want or need most of them, suggesting the hierarchy is wrong.

smells.name: Comments
smells.description: Comments used as deodorant for bad code. When you feel the need to write a comment, first try to refactor so the comment becomes superfluous.
